lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Now is the winter of our discontent

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Made glorious summer by this sun of York;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And all the clouds that lour’d upon our house

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: In the deep bosom of the ocean buried.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Now are our brows bound with victorious wreaths;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Our bruised arms hung up for monuments;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Our stern alarums changed to merry meetings,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Our dreadful marches to delightful measures.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Grim-visaged war hath smooth’d his wrinkled front;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And now, instead of mounting barbed steeds

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To fright the souls of fearful adversaries,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: He capers nimbly in a lady’s chamber

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To the lascivious pleasing of a lute.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But I, that am not shaped for sportive tricks,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Nor made to court an amorous looking-glass;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I, that am rudely stamp’d, and want love’s majesty

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To strut before a wanton ambling nymph;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I, that am curtail’d of this fair proportion,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Cheated of feature by dissembling nature,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Deform’d, unfinish’d, sent before my time

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Into this breathing world, scarce half made up,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And that so lamely and unfashionable

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That dogs bark at me as I halt by them;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Why, I, in this weak piping time of peace,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Have no delight to pass away the time,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Unless to spy my shadow in the sun

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And descant on mine own deformity:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And therefore, since I cannot prove a lover,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To entertain these fair well-spoken days,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I am determined to prove a villain

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And hate the idle pleasures of these days.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Plots have I laid, inductions dangerous,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: By drunken prophecies, libels and dreams,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To set my brother Clarence and the king

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: In deadly hate the one against the other:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And if King Edward be as true and just

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: As I am subtle, false and treacherous,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: This day should Clarence closely be mew’d up,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: About a prophecy, which says that G

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Of Edward’s heirs the murderer shall be.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Dive, thoughts, down to my soul: here Clarence comes.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: His majesty,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Tendering my person’s safety, hath appointed

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: This conduct to convey me to the Tower.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Upon what cause?

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Because my name is George.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Alack, my lord, that fault is none of yours;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: He should, for that, commit your godfathers:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: O, belike his majesty hath some intent

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That you shall be new-christen’d in the Tower.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But what’s the matter, Clarence? may I know?

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Yea, Richard, when I know; for I protest

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: As yet I do not: but, as I can learn,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: He hearkens after prophecies and dreams;

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: And from the cross-row plucks the letter g,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: And says a wizard told him that by g

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: His issue disinherited should be;

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: And, for my name of George begins with g,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: It follows in his thought that I am he.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: These, as I learn, and such like toys as these

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Have moved his highness to commit me now.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Why, this it is, when men are ruled by women:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: ’Tis not the king that sends you to the Tower;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: My Lady Grey his wife, Clarence, ’tis she

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That tempers him to this extremity.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Was it not she and that good man of worship,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Anthony Woodville, her brother there,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That made him send Lord Hastings to the Tower,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: From whence this present day he is deliver’d?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: We are not safe, Clarence; we are not safe.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: By heaven, I think there’s no man is secure

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: But the queen’s kindred and night-walking heralds

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: That trudge betwixt the king and Mistress Shore.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Heard ye not what an humble suppliant

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Lord hastings was to her for his delivery?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Humbly complaining to her deity

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Got my lord chamberlain his liberty.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I’ll tell you what; I think it is our way,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: If we will keep in favour with the king,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To be her men and wear her livery:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The jealous o’erworn widow and herself,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Since that our brother dubb’d them gentlewomen.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Are mighty gossips in this monarchy.

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: I beseech your graces both to pardon me;

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: His majesty hath straitly given in charge

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: That no man shall have private conference,

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: Of what degree soever, with his brother.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Even so; an’t please your worship, Brakenbury,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: You may partake of any thing we say:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: We speak no treason, man: we say the king

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Is wise and virtuous, and his noble queen

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Well struck in years, fair, and not jealous;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: We say that Shore’s wife hath a pretty foot,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: A cherry lip, a bonny eye, a passing pleasing tongue;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And that the queen’s kindred are made gentlefolks:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: How say you sir? Can you deny all this?

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: With this, my lord, myself have nought to do.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Naught to do with Mistress Shore! I tell thee, fellow,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: He that doth naught with her, excepting one,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Were best he do it secretly, alone.

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: What one, my lord?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Her husband, knave: wouldst thou betray me?

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: I beseech your grace to pardon me, and withal

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: Forbear your conference with the noble duke.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: We know thy charge, Brakenbury, and will obey.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: We are the queen’s abjects, and must obey.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Brother, farewell: I will unto the king;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And whatsoever you will employ me in,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Were it to call King Edward’s widow sister,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I will perform it to enfranchise you.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Meantime, this deep disgrace in brotherhood

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Touches me deeper than you can imagine.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: I know it pleaseth neither of us well.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Well, your imprisonment shall not be long;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I will deliver you, or else lie for you:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Meantime, have patience.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: I must perforce. Farewell.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Go, tread the path that thou shalt ne’er return,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Simple, plain Clarence! I do love thee so,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That I will shortly send thy soul to heaven,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: If heaven will take the present at our hands.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But who comes here? the new-deliver’d Hastings?

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Good time of day unto my gracious lord!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: As much unto my good lord chamberlain!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Well are you welcome to the open air.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: How hath your lordship brook’d imprisonment?

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: With patience, noble lord, as prisoners must:

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: But I shall live, my lord, to give them thanks

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: That were the cause of my imprisonment.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: No doubt, no doubt; and so shall Clarence too;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: For they that were your enemies are his,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And have prevail’d as much on him as you.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: More pity that the eagle should be mew’d,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: While kites and buzzards prey at liberty.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: What news abroad?

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: No news so bad abroad as this at home;

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: The king is sickly, weak and melancholy,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: And his physicians fear him mightily.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Now, by Saint Paul, this news is bad indeed.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: O, he hath kept an evil diet long,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And overmuch consumed his royal person:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: ’Tis very grievous to be thought upon.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: What, is he in his bed?

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: He is.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Go you before, and I will follow you.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: He cannot live, I hope; and must not die

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Till George be pack’d with post-horse up to heaven.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I’ll in, to urge his hatred more to Clarence,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: With lies well steel’d with weighty arguments;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And, if I fall not in my deep intent,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Clarence hath not another day to live:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Which done, God take King Edward to his mercy,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And leave the world for me to bustle in!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: For then I’ll marry Warwick’s youngest daughter.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: What though I kill’d her husband and her father?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The readiest way to make the wench amends

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Is to become her husband and her father:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The which will I; not all so much for love

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: As for another secret close intent,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: By marrying her which I must reach unto.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But yet I run before my horse to market:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Clarence still breathes; Edward still lives and reigns:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: When they are gone, then must I count my gains.

stage_directions.text: Enter Richard, Duke of Gloucester, solus.
Brother, good day: what means this armed guard That waits upon your grace?

glosses.definition: as in 'winter of our discontent' — the winter of our troubles. NOTE: the line means our winter has ENDED, turned to summer; it does NOT mean 'our troubles continue.' Richard is observing that York's struggles are over
Edward IV, Richard's elder brother, now king; the pun on 'son/sun' (homophones in Shakespeare's English) aligns the royal heir with the Yorkist sun-badge
frowned; hung threateningly (as storm-clouds over a house)

glosses.phrase: deep bosom of the ocean buried
glosses.definition: drowned in the ocean's depths — the Yorkist troubles are swallowed up like clouds falling into the sea
foreheads crowned with laurel garlands — the Roman triumphal image

glosses.phrase: bruised arms hung up for monuments
glosses.definition: battered weapons hung on the wall as war-trophies — arms now ornamental, not used
calls to arms; battle-trumpets
stately courtly dances
grim-faced war personified; war given a face
smoothed his furrowed forehead — war has relaxed its scowl
armored warhorses — 'barded' meant fitted with protective horse-armor
leaps gracefully — war, now idle, dances
suggestive, sensual pleasure-giving
playful amorous antics
a mirror used by lovers to admire themselves
crudely formed (as if by a defective coin-stamp)
flirtatious, softly-walking maiden — nymph = classical nature-spirit, here a courtly beauty
cut off from, deprived of a handsome shape

glosses.phrase: Cheated of feature by dissembling nature
glosses.definition: cheated of good looks by a deceiving Nature — personified Nature has tricked him
born prematurely
barely half-formed
limp
peaceful era marked by shepherd-pipes rather than war-trumpets
elaborate upon; discourse at length on (from the musical term for a counterpoint above a melody)
demonstrate myself a villain; Richard's decisive self-construction as the play's villain-protagonist
opening acts, preliminary schemes
defamatory written accusations
caged up (a term from falconry — to 'mew' a hawk was to confine it during moulting)
on account of a prophecy — King Edward is supposedly acting on a prediction
the alphabet (so called because old primers placed a cross before the letters)
trifles, silly notions
Queen Elizabeth (Woodville), Edward IV's commoner-born wife; Richard's epithet dismisses her royal title
Elizabeth's brother, Earl Rivers — Richard's scornful naming of a Woodville by first name is calculated insult
Jane Shore, Edward IV's favourite mistress — a London goldsmith's wife famous for her influence at court
wear her household's servant-uniform — i.e. serve the queen as retainers
worn-out widow — a sneer at the queen's previous marriage
powerful godmothers; here, powerful meddlers in state affairs
strictly commanded
well advanced in years — Gloucester's sarcastic praise of the still-young queen
things cast off; abject slaves — a coinage, pun on 'subjects'
set free, release from imprisonment
take your place in prison (also: lie on your behalf)
endured, tolerated
eagle should be caged — Hastings' bitter metaphor, echoing Richard's earlier 'mew'd up'
common birds of prey — base opportunists, here the queen's kin
Richard's characteristic oath; by the Apostle Paul
lived a dissolute life; indulged immoderately (blamed for Edward's illness)
relay horse used for fast travel — Richard wants George dispatched to heaven in a hurry
Anne Neville, widow of the murdered Edward (son of Henry VI); Richard plans to marry her

glosses.phrase: kill’d her husband and her father
glosses.definition: he killed Prince Edward (at Tewkesbury) and her father-in-law Henry VI — not her biological father; the line telegraphs the audacity of 1.2

glosses.phrase: run before my horse to market
glosses.definition: get ahead of myself (proverbial — counting one's profits before the transaction)

annotations.title: The opening that everyone misreads
annotations.body: Four of the most quoted lines in English — and the most misread. "Now is the winter of our discontent / Made glorious summer by this sun of York" does not mean "our troubles continue." It means the opposite: our winter has ENDED, turned to summer. The York dynasty has triumphed, the wars are done, and Richard is observing a world of peace. He cannot belong to it. His opening catalogue of disarmament — bruised arms hung up for monuments, stern alarums changed to merry meetings, grim-visaged war smoothing his wrinkled front — is not a lament for war but a register of his own obsolescence. When he turns at line 14 ("But I, that am not shaped for sportive tricks") the logic clicks: he will manufacture the conflict the world no longer provides. The speech is his explanation for everything that follows.
sources: Emrys Jones, Scenic Form in Shakespeare (1971)

annotations.title: Shakespeare's first villain-protagonist and the audience pact
annotations.body: Richard's direct address to us is Shakespeare's great theatrical innovation of the early 1590s. The Vice-figure of the old morality plays had winked at the audience; Richard extends the wink into a conspiracy. By the time he tells us "I am determined to prove a villain," we have already agreed to watch. Each of his soliloquies and asides reaffirms the pact: he confides in us before betraying everyone onstage. The technique is the template for Iago, Edmund, and Macbeth's darker soliloquies — but Richard does it first, and most audaciously. We are not simply observing a villain; we are his accomplices. The play's moral force depends on this complicity: when the ghosts arrive in 5.3, they arrive for us too. Coleridge called the device "a species of ventriloquism" by which Shakespeare makes the audience imagine and desire what Richard will do.
sources: Samuel Taylor Coleridge, Lectures on Shakespeare (1811–13)
Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)
Robert Weimann, Shakespeare and the Popular Tradition in the Theater (1967)

annotations.title: The deformity and the Tudor portrait
annotations.body: Richard's self-description — curtailed of fair proportion, cheated of feature by dissembling nature, halting, deformed — descends from a specific historical tradition. Thomas More's unfinished History of King Richard III (written c. 1513, published 1557) established the withered-hunchback portrait that Edward Hall (1548) and Raphael Holinshed (1577) then amplified, and which Shakespeare inherits directly. The historical Richard had severe scoliosis of the spine (confirmed in 2012 when his skeleton was rediscovered beneath a Leicester car park), but he was not the shriveled monster of the stage tradition: Tudor polemic shaped the portrait to legitimize the dynasty that had overthrown him. Virgil's job here is not to adjudicate. The dramatic Richard is inseparable from the propagandistic tradition that produced him, and Shakespeare is working with More's portrait, not the man. But readers should know that the portrait has a political origin, and that the twenty-first century has seen substantial historical revision.
sources: Thomas More, History of King Richard III (c. 1513)

annotations.title: The plot announced before it begins
annotations.body: Shakespeare structures Richard III unlike any of his earlier histories: the villain lays out the plot mechanism in the first forty lines, then executes it in public view. "Plots have I laid, inductions dangerous" is not a boast but a prospectus. The prophecy concerning the letter G, which Richard says "Of Edward's heirs the murderer shall be," is really about Gloucester himself, but he has engineered its use against Clarence (George). We are watching plotting-as-framing. This openness — the audience always knows more than the stage does — produces the play's signature tempo. Dramatic irony is not used occasionally here; it is the structural principle. Every scene until the coronation (4.2) operates under the rule that we see the instrument of Richard's next move before the characters do. The pleasure is watching them catch up, too late.
sources: Wolfgang Clemen, A Commentary on Shakespeare's Richard III (1968)
A. P. Rossiter, Angel with Horns (1961)

annotations.title: The quiet brutality of Clarence's farewell
annotations.body: "Go, tread the path that thou shalt ne'er return, / Simple, plain Clarence!" Richard says as his brother exits toward the Tower. What makes the line chilling is its tone — not triumph, almost affection. "I do love thee so, / That I will shortly send thy soul to heaven" is the play's first fully formed example of Richard's signature: sentimentality as weapon. Murder clothed in the language of care. The pattern will recur with the princes ("the sons of Edward sleep in Abraham's bosom", 4.3) and in the wooing of Anne ("He that bereft thee, lady, of thy husband, / Did it to help thee to a better husband", 1.2). Each time, Richard uses the idiom of benevolence to describe destruction. This is not simply hypocrisy — it is a specific rhetorical strategy that would become Shakespeare's template for evil eloquence across the major tragedies.

annotations.title: The Q1 and F1 texts of Richard III
annotations.body: Richard III survives in two substantively different texts: the First Quarto (Q1, 1597) and the First Folio (F1, 1623). F1 is roughly two hundred lines longer, with the "clock scene" (4.2) extended and several passages elsewhere expanded — including small additions to this final soliloquy. Unlike King Lear's Q1/F1 situation, which most modern editions treat as two distinct works, Richard III's variants are conventionally regarded as stages of the same play, and editors usually conflate them following F1's fuller text. Standard Ebooks uses a conflated modern-editorial text. Most passages students encounter are common to both versions, but the editorial question of which "Richard III" a production stages is live. Virgil raises this here as a small flag: the text is historically unstable, and that instability is part of what makes Shakespearean editing a living scholarly discipline.
sources: John Jowett, The Oxford Shakespeare: Richard III (2000)
Antony Hammond, Arden Shakespeare: Richard III (1981)

trials.prompt: What does "Now is the winter of our discontent / Made glorious summer by this sun of York" actually mean?
options: Our troubles continue, and our house of York is suffering
Our winter has ended and turned to summer; our troubles are over
Edward IV (the sun of York) is the source of our discontent
The seasons have unnaturally reversed, signaling doom for the realm

trials.prompt: Why does Richard say he is "determined to prove a villain"?
options: He has been slandered and wishes to live up to his bad reputation
God has predestined him to villainy and he cannot escape it
He cannot belong to the peaceful court world and will manufacture conflict for himself
He wishes to avenge the wrongs done to the House of York

trials.prompt: What is distinctive about Richard's direct address to the audience in this scene?
options: It follows the choral convention inherited from Greek tragedy
It is a formal aside, isolated from the surrounding dialogue
It makes the audience complicit in his schemes — we agree to watch him plot before he acts
It is a conventional prologue, informing the audience of prior events

trials.prompt: What is Richard's plan against his brother Clarence?
options: To have him exiled to Burgundy
To turn King Edward against him using a prophecy about the letter G, leading to Clarence's imprisonment
To challenge him openly to single combat
To force him to renounce his dukedom in exchange for safety

trials.prompt: When Richard says of Clarence "I do love thee so, / That I will shortly send thy soul to heaven," what is the rhetorical effect?
options: Genuine brotherly affection mingled with regret
Sentimentality repurposed as a weapon — the idiom of care describing murder
A sarcastic refusal of the royal order to imprison Clarence
Religious consolation offered to a condemned brother

trials.prompt: Why does Richard want to marry "Warwick's youngest daughter" (Anne)?
options: He has loved her since childhood
Warwick's daughter will bring a large dowry
"Not all so much for love" but for "another secret close intent" — political, not romantic
His brother King Edward has commanded it as a peace-making alliance

trials.prompt: What is unusual about how Richard III opens, compared to most Shakespeare plays?
options: The play begins in the middle of an ongoing battle
The villain-protagonist enters alone and lays out the entire plot mechanism in advance
A chorus introduces the historical background before any character speaks
The opening is a dumb-show (mimed action without dialogue)

trials.prompt: The exaggerated deformity Richard ascribes to himself ("curtail'd of fair proportion," "deform'd, unfinish'd") descends from which historical tradition?
options: Eyewitness accounts from Richard's contemporaries, largely accurate
Tudor-era chronicles (More, Hall, Holinshed) that blackened Richard's image to legitimize the Tudor dynasty
Roman-era sources Shakespeare accessed via Plutarch
A Lancastrian propaganda tradition dating to the 1450s
